title: The King v The Director of Planning & Anor Ex P. The Proprietors, Strata Plan No. 46 (costs) (CL 21 of 2023) [2026] TCASC 21 (2 April 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF THE ACTION NO. CL 21/23
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
BETWEEN:
THE KING
-and
(1) THE DIRECTOR OF PHYSICAL PLANNING
(2) THE PHYSICAL PLANNING BOARD RESPONDENTS
Ex parte:
THE PROPRIETORS, STRATA PLAN NO. 46 APPLICANT
THE POINT LTD. INTERESTED PARTY
Before: The Hon. Mr Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Appearances: Mr Paul Dempsey of Dempsey & Co for the Applicants
Ms Clemar Hippolyte Principal Crown Counsel for the
Respondents
Mr Stephen Wilson KC of Wilson Wells for the Interested
Party
Hearing Date: 10th July 2024
Venue: Court 5, Graceway Plaza, Providenciales
JUDGMENT (COSTS)
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 1 of 28

1. On 7 June 2024, I handed down the substantive judgment dismissing the application for
judicial review.
2. The Applicant had been represented by Mr Peter McKnight at the substantive hearing, but
did not attend the handing down of the judgment. Mr Dempsey appeared on behalf of the
Applicant, having been instructed when the handing down was listed. Given the last-
minute instructions, Mr Dempsey was understandably not in a position to deal with the
issue of costs and the matter was adjourned to 10 July 2024.
3. Mr Wilson KC had made it clear in his outline submissions filed for the substantive
hearing that if successful, the Interested Party would be seeking an order for costs to be
taxed on the indemnity basis.
4. Dempsey & Co filed a Notice of Change of Attorney on 8 July 2024, formerly coming on
record as attorneys for the Applicant, and subsequently on 9 July 2024, filed a skeleton
argument on the issue of costs.
5. At the hearing on 7 June 2024, Ms Hippolyte made brief submissions to the effect that
unless there was some good reason to depart, the proper course of action was that costs
followed the event, and that the Court would only depart from that principle in judicial
review proceedings, if there was some wide public interest point which had been resolved
in the action and despite the outcome, clarity would have been achieved. She submitted
that the action had been pursued by the Applicant for personal/commercial reasons and
was proprietary in nature; that there was no public-interest element, and therefore no
reason to depart from the general rule; however, having reconsidered her position in light
of consideration of the written judgment, she adopted Mr Wilson KC’s application that
the costs should be awarded on the indemnity basis, due to the proceedings being
misconceived and unmeritorious.
6. Whilst Mr Dempsey, in the final paragraph of his skeleton argument, invites the Court to
make no order as to costs, I cannot accept that that is a genuine submission, as firstly, the
entirety of Mr Dempsey’s skeleton argument (save for some small submission that clarity
has been achieved with respect to two issues, as to which, see paragraphs 13 to 15 below)
is directed as to why the Court should not award indemnity costs, not that there should be
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 2 of 28

no order for costs, and secondly, in his oral submission he conceded that the Applicant
was not going to avoid all costs.
7. Mr Dempsey then, in the alternative, suggests that the costs liability should only arise after
24 May 2023. The reason for the reference to that date is as follows.
The Argument for Indemnity Costs
8. The primary basis for the claim for assessment to be on an indemnity basis arises from a
letter sent by the Interested Party’s attorneys dated 24 May 2023. That letter
comprehensively set out the Interested Party’s position with respect to concerns raised by
the Applicant, and, concessions made as a result of communications between the
Interested Party and the Applicant, addressing the Applicant’s concerns. In that letter, the
Interested Party’s position was as follows:
“… Your client’s case is bound to fail. The decision of the Physical Planning
Board to grant conditional development permission is an entirely reasonable
and proper one, made in accordance with the provisions of the Physical
Planning Ordinance and its subsidiary legislation, and is unassailable from a
public law point of view. We are therefore writing to give your client the
opportunity, ahead of the hearing listed for Friday, 26 May 2023, to discontinue
its misconceived action in return for which our client will not seek an order for
payment of its costs.”
The letter concluded:
“This letter is intended to be open correspondence. Should your client decline
to take advantage of the generous offer contained herein, our clients shall
naturally refer the Court to the contents of this letter on the issue of costs.”
9. In closing his outline submissions, Mr Wilson KC submits:
“The grounds on which the courts will review the exercise of an administrative
discretion are: abuse of power, e.g. bad faith; a mistake in construing the limits
of the power; a procedural irregularity; or unreasonableness in the
Wednesbury sense, i.e. unreasonableness verging on an absurdity. The
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 3 of 28

Applicant has failed to demonstrate any such grounds.
…
In all the circumstances, this Honourable Court is asked to hold that it was
correct and reasonable of the Board to grant the ODP and the Consolidation
and to dismiss the Application with costs on the indemnity basis for those costs
incurred after 24 May 2023.”
10. Ms Hippolyte adopts Mr Wilson KC’s arguments and says further that the manner in
which the litigation was handled makes the matter suitable for an indemnity costs order.
11. Mr Wilson KC submits that there are 3 applications by the Interested Party with respect
to costs:
a. Whether the Interested Party is entitled to seek its costs?
b. Whether costs should be awarded on the indemnity basis in light of the letter of 24
May 2023 and the overall conduct of the litigation? and
c. Whether Mr McKnight should be made to pay the costs personally?
Liability as to Costs
12. The first issue that arises is whether the Applicant should bear the costs of this failed
judicial review. The basic principle is set out in Order 62 r.3(3) of the Civil Rules 2000
which provides:
If the Court in the exercise of its discretion sees fit to make any order as to the
cost of any proceedings, the Court shall order the costs to follow the event, except
when it appears to the Court that in the circumstances of the case some other order
should be made as to the whole or any part of the costs.
13. Mr Dempsey identifies that there were two issues of particular concern that were raised
in the judgment. The first was the correctness of allowing the consolidation of
noncontiguous parcels of land separated by a roadway, and the second was whether the
proposed development would be 7 or 8 storeys.
14. Mr Dempsey submits that as the Court expounded on both of these issues, it “added to the
sum of public law and has application and impact outside of the instant case.”
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 4 of 28

15. Insofar that Mr Dempsey was making an argument that there was a public interest angle
in the application, not something that was suggested by Mr McKnight at trial, then I take
the view that the interest in the application was most definitely driven by the interests of
the applicant and those representing it.
16. Ms Hippolyte directs me to Davey v Aylesbury Vale District Council1 in which Sir
Anthony Clarke MR said2
“I entirely agree with the guidelines set out by Sedley LJ at para 21 above. I
would however add one note of caution. It does seem to me that costs should
ordinarily follow the event and that it is for the Claimant who has lost to show
that some different approach should be adopted on the facts of a particular
case. That principle is supported by the decision and reasoning of Dyson J in
R v Lord Chancellor ex parte Child Poverty Action Group [1998] 2 All ER 755,
[1999] 1 WLR 347 at 355H-356E, [1998] NLJR 205. That passage concludes
as follows:
“. . . In considering whether, and in what circumstances, there should
be a departure from the basic rule that costs follow the event in public
interest challenge cases, in my view it is important to have in mind the
rationale for that basic rule, and that it is for the Applicants to show
why, exceptionally, there should be a departure from it.”
The basic rule he refers to is, as he explained at p 356C, that costs follow the
event in public law cases, as in others, because, where an unsuccessful claim is
brought against a public body, it imposes costs on that body which have to be
met out of money diverted from the funds available to fulfil its primary public
functions. (My emphasis)
17. The above was set out as a guideline by Sedley LJ3 as being:
“On the conclusion of full judicial review proceedings in a defendant's favour,
1 [2007] EWCA Civ 1166.
2 At para. [29].
3 At para. [21].
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 5 of 28

the nature and purpose of the particular claim is relevant to the exercise of the
judge's discretion as to costs. In contrast to a judicial review claim brought
wholly or mainly for commercial or proprietary reasons, a claim brought partly
or wholly in the public interest, albeit unsuccessful, may properly result in a
restricted or no order for costs.”
18. I was also referred to De Smith’s Judicial Review4 in which the learned authors take a
similar view as follows:
“Costs following the full hearing of a claim for judicial review will generally
be awarded to the successful party. In some circumstances, however, it may be
inappropriate for the unsuccessful claimant to be ordered to meet the
defendant’s costs, where the claim was brought not with a view to personal gain
and there was some wider public interest involved.”
19. Mr Wilson KC directs me to R (on the application of Smeaton) -v- Secretary of State
for Health5 in which Munby J, dismissing the case, considered whether the sale of the
‘morning after pill’ by pharmacists without prescription raised an issue that was of public
importance, said:6
“I do not doubt that there are judicial review cases where public interest or
analogous considerations will make it inappropriate in all the circumstances to
require even a wholly unsuccessful claimant to pay the defendant's costs. But
in my judgment this is simply not such a case.
In New Zealand Maori Council and Others v Attorney-General of New Zealand
and Others [1994] 1 AC 466, at 485F Lord Woolf said this:
'There remains the question of costs. Although the appeal is to be
dismissed, the applicants were not bringing the proceedings out of any
motive of personal gain. They were pursuing the proceedings in the
4 Sweet & Maxwell 7 ed.
5 [2002] EWHC 610 (Admin), [2002] 2 FLR 146, [2002] 2 FCR 193, 66 BMLR 59, (2002) Times, 2 May, [2002] All ER
(D) 115 (Apr).
6 At paras. [410-411].
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 6 of 28

interest of taonga which is an important part of the heritage of New
Zealand. Because of the different views expressed by the members of the
Court of Appeal on the issues raised on this appeal, an undesirable lack
of clarity inevitably existed in an important area of the law which it was
important that their Lordships examine and in the circumstances their
Lordships regard it as just that there should be no order as to the costs
on this appeal.'
20. He continued:7
“I derive from Lord Woolf's words two points of significance for present
purposes: first, that in considering what order for costs to make it is important
to have regard to the fact, if fact it be, that the proceedings were not brought
out of any motive of personal gain and, secondly, that it is in the same way
important to have regard to any wider public interest which may be involved. I
have not the slightest difficulty with either of these propositions.” (My
emphasis)
21. He then went on to consider R v Lord Chancellor ex parte Child Poverty Action
Group; R -v- Director of Public Prosecutions, ex parte Bull and another8 where 353G
Dyson J said:
“I should start by explaining what I understand to be meant by a public interest
challenge. The essential characteristics of a public law challenge are that it
raises public law issues which are of general importance, where the applicant
has no private interest in the outcome of the case. It is obvious that many, indeed
most judicial review challenges, do not fall into the category of public interest
challenges so defined. This is because, even if they do raise issues of general
importance, they are cases in which the applicant is seeking to protect some
private interest of his or her own. The central submission advanced on behalf
of the applicants is that, because of those essential characteristics, the court
7 At para. [412].
8 [1998] 2 All ER 755.
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 7 of 28

should be more willing to make no order as to costs against an unsuccessful
applicant in public interest challenge cases than in other cases. It is submitted
that public interest challenges are not “ordinary litigation” between adverse
parties.' (My emphasis)
22. Save for the suggestion by Mr Dempsey that there was some wider public interest as the
issues resolved may have wider impact, for the reasons I have detailed, this case was not
a public interest case. Mr Dempsey’s suggestion that the judgment has brought clarity to
the tension I observed between the definition of ‘storey’ in the Building Code and that in
the Development Manual and the National Physical Development Plan 2020, arose as a
result of my analysis of the case and not as a result of any issue or argument raised by the
Applicant, and, cannot be said to be more than an issue of general importance at best.
Also, I find difficulty in the suggestion that the question of whether it is legally possible
to consolidate parcels of land separated by a road parcel raises the interests of the general
public at all. Even if it does, again, it cannot be more than a matter of general importance
at its highest. Ms Hippolyte submits that no point has been clarified that assists the
jurisdiction in any substantive way. I agree.
23. I have heard very little argument why the general principle should be displaced and that
the Applicant should not pay the costs of this failed application for judicial review, and
indeed the Applicant accepts it will be burdened to some extent with a costs order against
it. I therefore hold that the Applicant shall be subject to an order for costs, but that does
not dispose of the matter.
The Interested Party’s Costs
24. A point arises, that was not addressed by Mr Dempsey nor Ms Hippolyte, but was raised
by Mr Wilson KC in relation to the Interested Party’s costs. The learned authors of
Supperstone, Goudie and Walker on Judicial Review9 comment as follows:
“The general rule is that an unsuccessful claimant is not required to pay for
more than one set of costs. Where several parties appear to defend a judicial
9 LexisNexis maintained Ed. at [20.119].
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 8 of 28

review, they will usually only be allowed one set of costs between them.
However, the court may order the payment of further costs in certain
circumstances. It is only where an interested party can show that there was
likely to be a separate issue on which he was entitled to be heard, or if he has
a particular interest which requires separate representation, that the court will
order a 'second set of costs'. However, even a separate interest and an
entitlement to separate representation may not be sufficient to warrant the
grant of a second set of costs in the absence of a conflicting interest which must
be promoted … The courts have tended to allow two sets of costs more
frequently where the claimant to the judicial review is a company rather than
an individual of limited means. The court may also consider whether an
interested party could have filed evidence but left it to the first defendant to
make submissions.”
25. They continue10:
“Where an interested party has a special interest in a matter, such as that his
liberty is at stake, the courts will make him an award of costs. The court may
also consider that a second set should be ordered where the interests of the
parties are complex such as to justify the need for them both to appear.
26. Despite the lack of any opposition to both the Respondent and the Interested Party
claiming their costs, I am of the view that I should consider whether this is a proper case
in which the Applicant should be burdened with a second set of costs.
27. Mr Wilson KC refers me again to Munby J in Smeaton where at paragraph 431 he says:
“Mr Gordon submits that SPUC should not be ordered to pay any part of
Schering's costs. I do not agree.
Mr Gordon submits – correctly – that the normal rule, even in what he calls
ordinary cases (that is, cases which do not involve a public interest challenge),
is that two sets of respondents' costs are not awarded against an unsuccessful
10 Ibid at [20.120).
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 9 of 28

claimant for judicial review. Mr Anderson agrees but says that a second set of
costs may be awarded if justified in the special circumstances of the particular
case.
28. It was agreed in Smeaton that the relevant principles were to be found in the speech of
Lord Lloyd of Berwick in Bolton Metropolitan District Council and Others -v-
Secretary of State for the Environment; Bolton Metropolitan District Council and
Others -v- Manchester Ship Canal Co; Bolton Metropolitan District Council and
Others -v- Trafford Park Development Corporation (Practice Note).11 Munby J
said:12
“In that case the House of Lords had to consider the proper approach to the
award of costs where there is multiple representation of ultimately successful
parties in statutory planning appeals but the same principles apply to judicial
review cases.”
29. He then went on to quote Lord Lloyd of Bewick at 1178E:
'What then is the proper approach? As in all questions to do with costs, the
fundamental rule is that there are no rules. Costs are always in the discretion
of the court, and a practice, however widespread and longstanding, must never
be allowed to harden into a rule. But the following propositions may be
supported:
(1) The Secretary of State, when successful in defending his decision,
will normally be entitled to the whole of his costs. He should not be
required to share his award of costs by apportionment, whether by
agreement with other parties, or by further order of the court. Insofar
as the Court of Appeal in the Wychavon District Council case may
have encouraged or sanctioned such a course, I would respectfully
disagree.
(2) The developer will not normally be entitled to his costs unless he
11 [1995] 1 WLR 1176.
12 At para. [432].
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 10 of 28

can show that there was likely to be a separate issue on which he was
entitled to be heard, that is to say an issue not covered by counsel for
the Secretary of State; or unless he has an interest which requires
separate representation. The mere fact that he is the developer will not
of itself justify a second set of costs in every case.
(3) A second set of costs is more likely to be awarded at first instance,
than in the Court of Appeal or House of Lords, by which time the issues
should have crystallised, and the extent to which there are indeed
separate interests should have been clarified.
(4) An award of a third set of costs will rarely be justified, even if there
are in theory three or more separate interests.
On the facts of the present case the Secretary of State is clearly entitled to the
whole of his costs. The only question is whether the Manchester Ship Canal Co
[the developer] should also receive their costs. In my opinion they should. I
accept that the issues were all capable of being covered by counsel for the
Secretary of State. But the case has a number of special features.'
30. Mr Wilson KC submits that this was a case which was a direct attack on the Interested
Party developer. It was its interest that would be directly affected if the judicial review
had quashed the outline development permission.
31. Throughout the substantive hearing, Mr McKnight was asked to detail what it was he was
saying the Physical Planning Board had got wrong or how it had erred in its approach to
the application, and was unable to do so, which left the Court with the view that the intent
was to stop the development at all costs, rather than correct any procedural error.
32. The case was approached on the basis the Applicant simply did not want the development
to go ahead. This was demonstrated by the fact that on the application for leave to move
for judicial review, the Court was not advised as to the concessions made by the Interested
Party, following meetings between the Applicant’s representatives and the developer. The
Court and indeed the Respondent were not referred to the letter of 24 May 2023. As Mr
Wilson KC submits, the Interested Party had no choice but to intervene. Had there been
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 11 of 28

no intervention, then the efforts made by the Interested Party to placate the Applicant’s
concerns would not have been put before the Court.
33. The question I have considered is whether this matter could have been adequately dealt
with by the Interested Party putting in evidence and allowing Ms Hippolyte to deal with
arguments and submissions. Mr Dempsey accepts that the Interested Party raised issues
that would not have been dealt with by the Respondent. The Respondent was charged with
responding to errors or omissions in the decision-making process of the Physical Planning
Board. The Interested Party’s interest was distinct and very different.
34. Mr Wilson KC adopts the submissions of Mr Anderson KC, counsel for Schering (an
interested party in Smeaton), where he says:13
“… that Schering's evidence and submissions were not duplicative. He says,
correctly, that Schering did not seek to duplicate the evidence of Ms Walsh on
nineteenth-century medical and contraceptive matters, nor to duplicate the oral
submissions on behalf of the Secretary of State.”
35. I have to agree with that submission. The approach by Ms Hippolyte was to concentrate
on the process and decision of the Physical Planning Board. Mr Wilson KC dealt with the
specific concerns raised by the Applicant in relation to the overall development itself,
which were pursued in light of the Interested Party’s accommodations and evidence. There
was no substantive duplication, but different issues were raised, which greatly assisted the
Court in determining the multiplexity of challenges to the development plans raised by
the Applicant, rather than any challenge to the decision-making process, non-such being
identified by the Applicant. In doing so, it allowed for a full judgment on all the issues to
be delivered and potentially closed a door to appeal. In saying that, I mean no disrespect
to Ms Hippolyte, as her focus was properly directed to the decision-making process and
not to the decision itself.
36. To borrow from Mumby J in Smeaton, standing back, and looking at the case in the round,
I ask myself: is it fair, just and appropriate having regard to all the aspects raised by the
13 At para. [436(iv)]
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 12 of 28

Applicant to order it to pay the costs of the Interested Party as well as the Respondent? In
my judgment, it is.
Indemnity Costs
37. Mr Wilson KC suggests that, because of the way the Applicant conducted the litigation of
this matter, it would be appropriate for the costs to be taxed on the indemnity basis, an
argument adopted by Ms Hippolyte, on the basis, she submits, that the application was
misconceived.
38. Ms Hippolyte refers to Three Rivers District Council and others -v- Bank of England14
in which Tomlinson J explained the indemnity basis of assessment as:15
“The significance of costs being ordered to be paid on an indemnity as opposed
to the standard basis is that, although the beneficiary of such an order will still
only be paid costs which have been reasonably incurred, there is no
requirement of proportionality and in cases of doubt on assessment it is for the
payer to show that the costs were not reasonably incurred. Whilst an indemnity
costs order does carry at least some stigma the purpose of such an order is not
to punish the paying party but to give a more fair result for the party in whose
favour a costs order is made – see Petrotrade Inc v Texaco Ltd (Note)[2002] 1
WLR 947, per Lord Woolf MR, at p. 949 and Victor Kermit Kiam II v MGN
Ltd [2002] EWCA Civ 66 at para 12 per Simon Brown LJ.”
39. He goes on to say:16
“One reason why it is appropriate to award indemnity costs is because there is
some circumstance which takes the case out of the norm – see per Lord Woolf
CJ in Excelsior Commercial and Industrial Holdings Ltd v Salisbury Hammer
Aspden and Johnson (a Firm) [2002] EWCA Civ 879 at para 19.”
40. Having then gone on to consider the authorities, he sets out the principles which should
guide the court’s determination as to whether indemnity costs should be awarded as
14 [2006] EWHC 816 (Comm); [2006] 5 Costs LR 714.
15 At para. [14].
16 At para. [24].
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 13 of 28

follows:
“(1) The court should have regard to all the circumstances of the case and the
discretion to award indemnity costs is extremely wide.
(2) The critical requirement before an indemnity order can be made in the
successful defendant’s favour is that there must be some conduct or some
circumstance which takes the case out of the norm.
(3) Insofar as the conduct of the unsuccessful claimant is relied on as a ground
for ordering indemnity costs, the test is not conduct attracting moral
condemnation, which is an a fortiori ground, but rather unreasonableness.
(4) The court can and should have regard to the conduct of an unsuccessful
claimant during the proceedings, both before and during the trial, as well as
whether it was reasonable for the claimant to raise and pursue particular
allegations and the manner in which the claimant pursued its case and its
allegations.
(5) Where a claim is speculative, weak, opportunistic or thin, a claimant who
chooses to pursue it is taking a high risk and can expect to pay indemnity costs
if it fails.
(6) A fortiori, where the claim includes allegations of dishonesty, let alone
allegations of conduct meriting an award to the claimant of exemplary
damages, and those allegations are pursued aggressively inter alia by hostile
cross examination.
(7) Where the unsuccessful allegations are the subject of extensive publicity,
especially where it has been courted by the unsuccessful claimant, that is a
further ground.
(8) The following circumstances take a case out of the norm and justify an order
for indemnity costs, particularly when taken in combination with the fact that a
defendant has discontinued only at a very late stage in proceedings;
(a) Where the claimant advances and aggressively pursues serious and
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 14 of 28

wide ranging allegations of dishonesty or impropriety over an extended
period of time;
(b) Where the claimant advances and aggressively pursues such
allegations, despite the lack of any foundation in the documentary
evidence for those allegations, and maintains the allegations, without
apology, to the bitter end;
(c) Where the claimant actively seeks to court publicity for its serious
allegations both before and during the trial in the international,
national and local media;
(d) Where the claimant, by its conduct, turns a case into an
unprecedented factual enquiry by the pursuit of an unjustified case;
(e) Where the claimant pursues a claim which is, to put it most
charitably, thin and, in some respects, far-fetched;
(f) Where the claimant pursues a claim which is irreconcilable with the
contemporaneous documents;
(g) Where a claimant commences and pursues large-scale and
expensive litigation in circumstances calculated to exert commercial
pressure on a defendant, and during the course of the trial of the action,
the claimant resorts to advancing a constantly changing case in order
to justify the allegations which it has made, only then to suffer a
resounding defeat.
41. Mr Wilson KC refers me to Kara Louise Browne -v- Ciliam Limited17 in which
indemnity costs were sought by the wholly successful defendant, in which 2 offers of
settlement had been made. Ramsay-Hale CJ rejected the defendant’s argument, in essence,
that failure by a Plaintiff not to accept an offer made by a defendant, attracted indemnity
costs. Ramsay-Hale CJ relied on Kiam -v- MGN Ltd (No. 2)18 and compared the position
17 (CL 163 of 2013) [2017] TCASC 16 (24 April 2017).
18 [2002] 4 All ER 861.
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 15 of 28

under Part 36 Civil Procedure Rules (England and Wales) (‘CPR’) with Order 62 r.3(4)
Civil Rules 2000 (Turks and Caicos Islands) and said;
“9. There is a dearth of authority setting out the principles on which the Court
should exercise its jurisdiction under Order 62 r.3(4) to award indemnity costs.
The learned editors of the White Book observe that it is not possible to define
the exact circumstances in which indemnity costs might be ordered: note
62/12/1 but, in practice, as observed by Peter Gibson J in Raja v Rubin [2000]
Ch 274,
“The award of costs on an indemnity basis is normally reserved to cases
where the court wishes to indicate its disapproval of the conduct in the
litigation of a party against whom costs are awarded.”
10. The authorities establish that the threshold for awarding indemnity costs is
met where a party is guilty of moral turpitude, i.e. has acted with the lack of
moral probity or has behaved disgracefully. Instances where indemnity costs
have been awarded include where the losing party persisted in its defence ‘to
an extent which was totally and utterly unreasonable [and] where large parts
of the defence have been totally unrealistic from the start,’2 where the
‘defendant made an allegation of fraud which had failed in its entirety’3 but not
where an unmeritorious appeal had been pursued4 and could be ordered
without the court concluding that the losing parties lawyers had acted so as to
incur moral condemnation.5

42. Mr Dempsey, also referring to Browne, submits:
“… that nothing in the manner in which the case was conducted that (sic) would
bring the Applicant within the ambit of being “totally and utterly
unreasonable” or unreasonable to a high degree rather than, in hindsight being
either wrong or misguided (being the standard adopted by the Honourable

Chief Justice in Browne).”
43. Mr Wilson KC conceded that Browne was against him, submitting that it was brought to
the Court’s attention under his obligation to do so, but submitted that time had moved on
and referred me to Burgess and another -v- Lejonvarn19 where Coulson LJ (with whom
Rose LJ and Sir Jack Beatson agreed) held:
“But a defendant can seek an order for indemnity costs if he or she can show
that, in all the circumstances of the case, the claimant's refusal to accept that
offer was unreasonable such as to be 'out of the norm'. Moreover, if the
claimant's refusal to accept the offer comes against the background of a
speculative, weak, opportunistic or thin claim, then an order for indemnity costs
may very well be made. That is what happened in Excelsior.”
44. The reference to speculative, weak, opportunistic or thin claims is a reference to Three
Rivers;20 however, these cases were decided under the CPR, in particular Part 44.11 which
provides:
Court’s powers in relation to misconduct
44.11
(1) The court may make an order under this rule where –
(a) a party or that party’s legal representative, in connection with a summary or
detailed assessment, fails to comply with a rule, practice direction or court order;
or
(b) it appears to the court that the conduct of a party or that party’s legal
representative, before or during the proceedings or in the assessment proceedings,
was unreasonable or improper.
(2) Where paragraph (1) applies, the court may –
(a) disallow all or part of the costs which are being assessed; or
19 [2020] 4 All ER 461.
20 See para. 40 above, in particular, principle 5. See also Burgess and another v Lejonvarn [2020] a All ER 461.

(b) order the party at fault or that party’s legal representative to pay costs which
that party or legal representative has caused any other party to incur.
45. Mr Dempsey refers me to Dixon and another -v- Radley House Partnership (A Firm)
and others21 from which he draws the following propositions:
“(i) Indemnity costs are appropriate only where the conduct of a party is
“unreasonable to a high degree”. ‘Unreasonable’ in this context does not mean
merely wrong or misguided in hindsight.
(ii) The court must decide whether there is something in the conduct of the
action, or the circumstances of the case in general, which takes it out of the
norm in a way which justifies an order for indemnity costs.
(iii) The pursuit of a weak claim will not usually, on its own, justify an order
for indemnity costs, provided that the claim was at least arguable.
(iv) The pursuit of a hopeless claim, or a claim which the party pursuing it
should have realised was hopeless, may well lead to such an order.
(v) The degree of badness of a point may go into the scales when considering
whether indemnity costs should be awarded even though the mere fact that a
point has been rejected or would have been rejected on an application for
summary judgment is not of itself in the normal case [a] reason for awarding
indemnity costs.”
46. I am mindful that the authorities to which I have been referred are all post-CPR cases.
Order 62 r.3(4)22 provides:
The amount of his costs which any party shall be entitled to recover is the amount
allowed after taxation on the standard basis where-
(a) an order is made that the costs of one party to proceedings be paid by
the other party to those proceedings, or
…
21 [2016] EWHC 3485 (QB).
22 The Civil Rules 2000.

unless it appears to the Court to be appropriate to order costs to be taxed on the
indemnity basis.
47. Note 62/3/723 states:
“… For indemnity costs to be awarded there should be some additional factor
warranting an order for costs on the indemnity basis.”
48. I refer to Note 62/12/124 where the learned writers say:
“It is not possible to define the exact circumstances in which indemnity costs
might be ordered. It is a matter, in each case of the judge exercising his
discretion on the facts before him. Where the Court found that the conduct of
the litigation was deserving of moral condemnation an order for costs on the
indemnity basis was appropriate.”
49. I refer back to Ramsay-Hale CJ in Browne:
“The authorities established that the threshold for awarding indemnity costs is
met where a party is guilty of moral turpitude, i.e. has acted with the lack of
moral probity or has behaved disgracefully.”
50. I give regard to the offer that was made by the Interested Party.25 This was not an offer to
settle the matter in what I would describe as a ‘normal claim’ i.e. an amount had been
offered by way of damages to settle the claim, but was an offer for the Applicant to
discontinue the proceedings with no claim for the legal costs of the Interested Party being
advanced.
51. Leave to move for judicial review was granted on 8 March 2023, the order granting leave
being perfected on 17 March 2023. The hearing on 26 May 2023, referred to in the letter
from the Interested Party’s attorneys, was a first mention hearing following the issue of
the originating motion to move for judicial review, which was filed on 30 March 2023
52. The claim was without doubt particularly weak, but pursuing a weak claim does not, on
23 Supreme Court Practice 1999 – The ‘White Book’.
24 See para. 41 above.
25 See para. 8 above.

its own, justify indemnity costs (Dixon). Whilst the claim could be catagorised as
misconceived, I am not of the view that the conduct of the Applicant was such that it could
be described as misconduct.
53. At the date of the offer, the only evidence that had been filed was that of the Applicant. I
have to consider whether the non-acceptance of the offer puts the case into the category
of ‘out of the norm’. I am conscious of the fact that the 24 May 2023 letter
comprehensively set out the Interested Party’s response to the concerns raised by the
Applicant, but I am of the view that it is a stretch too far to consider that the non-
acceptance of the offer, at that time was ‘totally and utterly unreasonable’ or amounted to
‘moral turpitude’ (Browne) or worthy of ‘moral condemnation’ (White Book Note
62/12/1).
54. I also bear in mind that I should consider all the circumstances of the case (Three Rivers)
and stand back to consider the overall fairness (Smeaton, albeit dealing with a second set
of costs, I do not see that the consideration of overall fairness should be so narrowly
limited, given the Court’s wide discretion when considering costs (Three Rivers)). I have
already allowed the Interested Party to recover costs. Cost orders are not intended to be
punitive in nature, and if I were to burden the Applicant both with two sets of costs and
award those costs on the indemnity basis, in circumstances where I do not see that the
Applicant’s case, although weak, amounted to something out of the norm, there would be
a strong argument that it was not a fair result, but that the additional burden was punitive.
Should Mr McKnight be ordered to pay the costs personally?
55. Mr Wilson raised the issue that Mr McKnight should personally pay for what he suggests
are the wasted costs of this action. He founds his application on the basis of submissions
that the claim was hopeless and speculative and should not have been advanced,
particularly in respect of the absence of any argument regarding the grounds on which
judicial reviews are founded.26
56. I was not addressed as to the authority to make wasted costs orders against a conducting

attorney in this jurisdiction, and accordingly, what follows is from my own research and
has not had the benefit of argument and is necessarily weaker as a result. Ord. 62 r.11(1)(a)
in the White Book provides:
Where the Court decides to make an order under section 51(6) of the Supreme
Court Act 1981 disallowing wasted costs or ordering a legal representative to
meet such costs or part of them, it shall, subject to paragraph 4, specify in the
order the costs which are to be so disallowed or met and may make such other
order as it thinks fit;
57. Paragraph 4 of the above provision provides:
No order may be made under section 51(6) unless the Court has given the legal
representative concerned a reasonable opportunity to appear and show cause why
an order should not be made, and I am mindful that Mr Dempsey appears on
behalf of the Applicant and not on behalf of Mr McKnight.
58. Note 62/11/3 says:
“The Court of Appeal has considered in detail the wasted cost jurisdiction
introduced by the Courts and Legal Services Act 1990 (Ridehalgh v. Horsefield;
Allen v. Unigate Dairies Ltd; Roberts v. Coverite (Asphalters)Ltd; Philex plc v.
Golban; Watson v. Watson; Antonelli v. Wade Gere Farr (A Firm) (1994) The
Times, January 28, CA. The court held that while litigants should not be
financially prejudiced by the unjustifiable conduct of litigation by their or their
opponent’s lawyers, the courts in the exercise of the wasted costs jurisdiction,
should be astute to control the threat of a new and costly form of satellite
litigation. When a wasted costs order was contemplated a three stage test
should be applied:
(a) Had the legal representative of whom complaint was made acted
improperly, unreasonably or negligently?
(b) If so, did such conduct cause the applicant to incur unnecessary
costs?

(c) If so, was it, in all the circumstances, just to order the legal

59. Subsections 51(6) and (7)27 of the Supreme Court Act 198128 (England and Wales)
provide:
(6) In any proceedings mentioned in subsection (1), the court may disallow, or
(as the case may be) order the legal or other representative concerned to meet, the
whole of any wasted costs or such part of them as may be determined in
accordance with rules of court.
(7) In subsection (6), “wasted costs” means any costs incurred by a party—
(a) as a result of any improper, unreasonable or negligent act or omission
on the part of any legal or other representative or any employee of such a
representative; or
(b) which, in the light of any such act or omission occurring after they
were incurred, the court considers it is unreasonable to expect that party
to pay.
60. Ord. 62 r.11 is omitted from the Civil Rules 2000, and there is no equivalent legislation
in the Turks and Caicos Islands.
61. In Elliott -v- Royal Bay Resort and Villas29 Ramsay-Hale CJ gave a ruling on the
jurisdictional point as a preliminary issue. She relied upon Myers -v- Elman30 and said
the following:
“When Myers v Elman was decided, the court’s wasted costs jurisdiction was
not regulated by the rules of the Supreme Court but subsequently a new rule –
which later became Order 62 rule 8(1) - was introduced which did regulate,
although not enlarge, this inherent jurisdiction.”
27 Inserted by section 4(1) of the Courts and Legal Services Act 1990.
28 Now the Senior Court Act 1981.
29 (CL 150 of 2008) [2015] TCASC 1 (15 October 2015).
30 [1940] A.C. 282; [1939] 4 All ER 484
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.

62. In fact, when Myers was decided, the Rules of the Supreme Court 1883 as amended to
1935 were in force31. Order LXV r.11 provided:
“If in any case it shall appear to the court or a judge that costs have been
improperly or without any reasonable cause incurred, or that by reason of any
undue delay in proceeding under any judgment or order, or any misconduct or
default of the solicitor, any costs properly incurred have nevertheless proved
fruitless to the person incurring the same, the court or judge may call on the
solicitor of a person by whom such costs have been so incurred to show cause
why such costs should not be disallowed as between the solicitor and his client,
and that also (if the circumstances of the case shall require) why the solicitor
should not repay to his client any costs which the client may have been ordered
to pay to any other person, and thereupon may make such order as the justice
of the case may require…”
63. In Myers, Viscount Maugham stated:32
“Order LXV., s.11, of the Rules of the Supreme Court provides the necessary
machinery where the person injured is the client of the solicitor. It is a rule
supplementary to the summary jurisdiction of the Court. It is not limited to
misconduct or default, but expressly extends to costs incurred improperly or
without reasonable cause, or which have proved fruitless by reason of undue
delay in proceeding under a judgment or order. The jurisdiction to order the
solicitor to pay costs to the opposite party is exercised on similar grounds.”
(Emphasis added)
64. In the same judgment, Lord Wright went on to say:
“… it is necessary to explain briefly the nature of the jurisdiction of the Court
which the appellant invoked. A solicitor (or in former days a solicitor or an
attorney) was long ago held to be an officer of the Court on the Roll of which
he was entered and as such to be subject to the discipline of that Court. The
31 England and Wales.
32 At [289].
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 23 of 28

Court might strike him off the Roll or suspend him; for instance, the Court of
Chancery might strike a solicitor off the Roll of the Court, and order a
communication of that order to be made to the Courts in Westminster Hall.
There are many such instances in the books. By the Solicitors Act, 1888, there
was established the Disciplinary Committee appointed by the Master of the
Rolls from members or past members of the Council of the Law Society. This
Committee was charged with the duty of investigating complaints against
solicitors and reporting their decision to the Court, which could then, if so
minded, strike the solicitor off the Roll or suspend him. It was not until 1919
that by the Solicitors Act of that year, the Disciplinary Committee was itself
given power to strike off the Roll or to suspend or to order payment of costs by
the solicitor subject to an appeal to the Court. But the jurisdiction of the Master
of the Rolls and any judge of the High Court over solicitors was expressly
preserved, as it now is by s. 5, sub-s. 1, of the Solicitors Act, 1932. Whether the
Court would now entertain an application to strike a solicitor off the Roll or to
suspend him instead of leaving the matter to the Disciplinary Committee may
be doubted. But alongside the jurisdiction to strike off the Roll or to suspend,
there existed in the Court the jurisdiction to punish a solicitor or attorney by
ordering him to pay costs, sometimes the costs of his own client, sometimes
those of the opposite party, sometimes, it may be, of both. The ground of such
an order was that the solicitor had been guilty of professional misconduct (as
it is generally called) not, however, of so serious a character as to justify
striking him off the Roll or suspending him. This was a summary jurisdiction
exercised by the Court which had tried the case in the course of which the
misconduct was committed. It was clearly preserved to the Court by s. 5, sub-s.
1, quoted above. It was a summary jurisdiction, in which the intervention of the
judge was invoked at the conclusion of the case either by motion in the
Chancery Court or by a motion or application for a rule in the Courts of
Common Law.
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)

65. Halsbury’s Laws of England33 also makes reference to the same inherent disciplinary
jurisdiction.
66. I therefore agree with Ramsay-Hale CJ where she holds in Elliott, the following:
“Having reviewed the authorities, I say that I accept Mr Nicholl’s submissions
and am satisfied that the Court retains its common law jurisdiction to make a
wasted costs order where the conduct complained of meets the standard of
culpability set out in Myers v Helms (sic).”
67. The question then for consideration is whether the conduct complained of in this matter
was such to meet that standard of culpability.
68. The primary thrust of the complaint is that the claim was hopeless and speculative and
should not have been advanced. Whilst the pursuit of a hopeless claim, or a claim which
the party pursuing it should have realised was hopeless, may well lead to a wasted costs
order under the CPR, I am not of the view that pursuing such a case in a pre-CPR regime
necessarily does.
69. Note 62/11/5 states:
“Pursuing a hopeless case- A personal representative was not held to have
acted improperly and unreasonably or negligently simply because he acted for
party who pursued a claim or defence which is plainly doomed to fail…”
70. In Myers, Viscount Maugham, in considering the threshold for making an order against
the conducting solicitor, was of the view:34
“The primary object of the court is not to punish the solicitor, but to protect the
client who has suffered and to indemnify the party who has been injured.”
71. He continued:35

Solicitors (5) Liabilities of Solicitors as Officers of the Senior Courts (i) Disciplinary Jurisdiction, Conduct and
Contempt 497. Disciplinary jurisdiction. And - Legal Professions (Volume 65 (2020), paras 201–514; Volume 66
(2020), paras 515–1079).
3. Solicitors (7) Professional Conduct, Practice and Redress (viii) Liability to Third Parties 610. Inherent jurisdiction.
34 At [289].

“I entirely agree with the contention that the jurisdiction in question ought to
be exercised only when there has been established a serious dereliction of duty
as a solicitor...”
72. In Myers, the wrongdoing was the filing of affidavits of documents known to be false. No
like charge is suggested in this matter.
73. In Gupta -v- Comer36 the court was tasked with considering whether the test to be applied
in an application under Ord. 62 r.11 was the same as that set out in Myers. Lord Donaldson
of Lymington M.R set out the issue as follows:
“Myers v. Elman [1940] A.C. 282 is authority for the proposition that in the
exercise of its inherent or common law jurisdiction over solicitors, as officers
of the Supreme Court, the court should not make a costs order against a
solicitor in his capacity as such, unless it was satisfied that the conduct which
gave rise to those costs being incurred could properly be described as "a
serious dereliction of duty as a solicitor either by himself or by his clerks" (per
Viscount Maugham, at p. 292), "gross negligence" (per Lord Atkin, at p. 304),
or "a gross neglect" (per Lord Wright, at p. 319). R.S.C., Ord. 65, r. 11 then in
force was treated as being intended to provide machinery for the exercise of
this inherent jurisdiction: see, at p. 289, per Viscount Maugham.
The Rules of the Supreme Court then in force had been made in 1883. A new
rule (Ord. 62, r. 8) was introduced in 1961, the wording of which was altered
in October 1966. All these rules were stated in the commentary contained in
successive editions of The Supreme Court Practice to be intended to provide
machinery for the exercise of the inherent jurisdiction of the court. The courts
in exercising the jurisdiction proceeded on the same basis in the sense that,
regardless of the precise wording of the rule for the time being in force, they
required that an applicant seeking an order for costs against a solicitor should
prove a serious dereliction of duty, gross negligence or gross neglect (see
Orchard v. South Eastern Electricity Board [1987] Q.B. 565 and the cases there
36 [1991] 1 Q.B. 629.
CL 21/23 – The King -v- (1) The Director of Planning (2) The Physical Planning Board Ex parte The Proprietors, Strata Plan No.
46 (The Point Ltd – Interested party)
Page 26 of 28

cited) thus applying the law as stated in Myers v. Elman [1940] A.C. 282. On
28 April 1986 a new rule was introduced in place of Ord. 62, r. 8, this being
the existing Ord. 62, r. 11. The original decision in Orchard's case was made
on 18 March 1985 and accordingly it was Ord. 62, r. 8 which was applicable,
notwithstanding that it reached the Court of Appeal in November 1986. I need
not reproduce Ord. 62, r. 11 since it is readily available in The Supreme Court
Practice 1991. Suffice it to say that its wording differs from that of its
predecessor in that it introduces references to "reasonable competence and
expedition" and omits references to "misconduct or default" which featured in
all the previous rules.” (Emphasis added)
74. He went on to consider Sinclair-Jones -v- Kay37 where it was held that the Myers criteria
were not applicable to the new rule. Balcombe LJ held:
“R.S.C., Ord. 62, r. 11(1) provides that where it appears to the court that costs
have been incurred unreasonably or improperly in any proceedings or have
been wasted by failure to conduct proceedings with reasonable competence and
expedition, the court may order (i) the solicitor whom it considers to be
responsible to repay to his client costs which the client has been ordered to pay
to any other party to the proceedings; or (ii) the solicitor personally to
indemnify such other parties against costs payable by them. The rule in this
form took effect on 28 April 1986 and replaced the old Ord. 62, r. 8 with
substantial amendments. The previous rule (and its predecessors) had been
held to do no more than cover the inherent jurisdiction of the court over
solicitors as its officers and, pursuant to the decision of the House of Lords in
Myers v. Elman [1940] A.C. 282, a solicitor could only be made to pay
personally the costs of the opposing party when it was clear that he was guilty
of a serious dereliction of duty or serious misconduct: see Orchard v. South
Eastern Electricity Board [1987] Q.B. 565. (Emphasis added).
75. Accordingly, in my judgment, in order to make a wasted costs order against the conducting
37 [1989] 1 W.L.R. 114.

attorney, it is necessary to show serious dereliction of duty or serious misconduct. I am
not of the view that in the circumstances of this case, that threshold has been met. I
therefore decline to invite Mr McKnight to make representations and to show cause why
such an order ought not to be made.
Disposition
76. The Plaintiff shall pay the costs of the Respondent and of the Interested Party, such costs
to be taxed on the standard basis if not agreed.
77. The application for a wasted costs order against Mr McKnight, the Applicant’s former
attorney, is refused.
